incorrects.description: Student selected A: 3 bags with 4 items each (an equal group)

steps.text: Look at A. Each bag has 4 items. That's the same in every bag. Equal groups have the same amount in each. Which picture shows different amounts in the containers?

incorrects.description: Student selected B: 4 circles with 2 dots each (an equal group)

steps.text: Look at B. Every circle has 2 dots. That's equal. For groups to NOT be equal, one group needs a different number. Which picture shows that?

incorrects.description: Student selected D: 5 bags with 3 items each (an equal group)

steps.text: Check D. All five bags have 3 items. That's the same number in each, so that's equal. Look for the picture where the containers have different amounts inside.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Option C highlights to show the correct answer.

steps.text: Let me show you. Look at A: 3 bags, each has 4. That's equal. B: 4 circles, each has 2. Equal. D: 5 bags, each has 3. Equal. Now C: one box has 3, the other has 5. Those are different numbers. That makes C NOT equal groups. The answer is C. Equal groups need the same amount in every container.

incorrects.description: Student selected 5 groups of 3: reversed the group structure

steps.text: You're counting what's inside the groups. Count the groups themselves first. How many bags are there? How many circles? How many symbols on the graph?

incorrects.description: Student selected 3 + 5: interpreted as addition instead of multiplication

params.description: All circles highlight showing repeated groups.

steps.text: Addition means we're combining two different amounts. Here, we have the same amount repeating. Each group has 5. Count how many groups have that same 5 inside.

incorrects.description: Student selected 15 things with no groups: seeing total without structure

params.description: Bags highlight to show container structure.

steps.text: The items are organized. They're not loose. Each bag holds 5, each circle holds 5, each symbol represents 5. How many of those containers are there?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System counts the 3 bags, then shows 5 items in each.

steps.text: Let me show you how to see this. First, I count the containers. 1 bag, 2 bags, 3 bags. Then I look inside one bag: 5 items. Every bag has that same 5. So it's 3 groups of 5. The circles show it the same way: 3 circles, 5 dots in each. The graph shows it too: 3 symbols, each symbol stands for 5. Different containers, same structure. All three show 3 groups of 5.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong container count on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. How many packs are on the shelf?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong container count on second attempt

params.description: Text '4 packs' highlights in the problem.

steps.text: Look at the problem. It says 4 packs of juice boxes. That tells you how many groups. Set the containers to match.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong container count three or more times. System models the answer.

steps.text: Let me show you. The problem says 4 packs of juice boxes. That means we need 4 groups. So I set the containers to 4. The number of packs is the number of groups.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong items per container on first attempt

steps.text: Check the problem. How many juice boxes in each pack?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong items per container on second attempt

params.description: Text 'Each pack has 6' highlights in the problem.

steps.text: The problem says each pack has 6 juice boxes. That's how many items go in each container. Read that part again and set the number.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong items per container three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System sets items per container to 6.

steps.text: Here's how this works. The problem says each pack has 6 juice boxes. That means every group needs the same amount, 6. So I set the items per container to 6. Now we have 4 groups of 6, which is what the shelf looks like. The answer is 6 in each pack.
